engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a definition of courage involving regular action in danger, settled disposition, and voluntariness. Response is an abstract meta-discussion about thoughts, causes, and knowledge that never mentions courage, danger, or any of the three conditions.

coherence.justification: Self-referential phrases like 'cause of a bad cause' and 'because the cause does not change the fact that a person may have a bad cause' loop and don't track. Ends mid-sentence ('So the thinking that the second idea is the only thing that matters is not').

substance.justification: No counterexample, distinction, or analogy targeting courage is given. The text restates vague claims about truth and causes without producing a case that satisfies the three conditions yet fails to be courage (or vice versa).

expressive_adequacy.justification: Vocabulary stays UGF-restricted, but the restriction is being used to dodge the actual concept rather than to express it. 'Idea/cause/true' substitute for the missing target concepts and the argument breaks rather than being clunky-but-recoverable.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a definition of political legitimacy via consent, freedom, and right to withdraw. Response talks vaguely about 'a way to do things well' and whether thinking something is best equals knowing it; politics, consent, and governance are absent.

coherence.justification: The two 'ideas' blur into each other ('the second idea says that thinking something is best is not the same as knowing it is best') and the chain 'first idea does not say... so second idea does not tell us' is non-sequitur.

substance.justification: No case is constructed. Generic skepticism about 'thinking best' rather than a scenario where the three legitimacy conditions hold but legitimacy is absent (or vice versa).

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF surface is maintained but the restriction prevents naming any political object; the argument never recovers a target. Clunky in the dismissive sense.

engagement.justification: Prompt is about diachronic identity via matter, form, and spatiotemporal continuity. Response is a generic 'first thing/second thing' meta-discussion of inference; objects, time, parts, and continuity are never touched.

coherence.justification: Opening sentence repeats 'the thing that is said' three times. The 'check each step' move is template without content and trails off ('the thinking is good when the first thing is true').

substance.justification: Offers no counterexample (e.g., Ship of Theseus, gradual replacement, fission). Pure procedural meta-talk about checking inferences.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF holds but at the cost of any referential anchor; no movable parts, no time index. Restriction is preventing the argument from forming.

engagement.justification: Prompt is a mereology definition (containment, exhaustion, non-overlap). Response is about gambling/choice under uncertainty ('pick a side if you know the chance is one half'), totally unrelated to wholes and parts.

coherence.justification: Self-contradicts: 'So, the first idea is not right' then 'So, the first idea is right' within four sentences. The 'counting/chance' frame and 'whole' framing slide past each other.

substance.justification: No part-whole counterexample (e.g., scattered objects, fuzzy boundaries, structural universals). Wrong domain entirely.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF maintained but argument never engages mereology; the restriction isn't the problem, the misreading is, and there is no recoverable target.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample to a definition of a chemical element (indecomposability, same proton count, natural occurrence on Earth). Response talks about strategy and guessing other players' moves; chemistry is absent.

coherence.justification: Reaches a conclusion ('a good way to pick a person because it works when we know the other person has the same thing they have') that doesn't connect to the setup; partial chain but no clear move.

substance.justification: Misses an easy counterexample (e.g., synthetic transuranic elements satisfy (i)+(ii) but not (iii); isotopes complicate (ii)). None of this appears.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF is fine surface-wise, but the response cannot connect to the chemical-element target at all; restriction not the bottleneck, but no recoverable argument.

engagement.justification: Prompt is about dispositional beliefs (assent on prompt, cognitive grounding, sincere affirmation on reflection). Response is about belief-maintenance and motivated reasoning, which touches the broader topic of belief but misses the dispositional structure and three conditions.

coherence.justification: Reads like a coherent paragraph about why people hold beliefs uncritically, but the 'first/then' frame doesn't track an argument; conclusion restates premise.

substance.justification: Names a phenomenon (motivated reasoning / 'people stop looking for the truth') but produces no counterexample to the definition — e.g., a tacit belief that fails (iii) on reflection, or assent-on-prompt without prior cognitive episode.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF feels mostly natural in this register and the move is recoverable, though 'in a near way' and 'lie' are doing a lot of work to gesture at 'self-deception'.

engagement.justification: Prompt is a topology definition (no partition into open sets, path-connectedness, integer-valued constant maps). Response is about 'ways of thinking that are better than other ways of thinking' — generic epistemology, no topology, no spaces, no continuity.

coherence.justification: The two 'true things' are restated in slightly different words rather than distinguished; the third paragraph is template advice ('we must look at how the parts fit together') without applying it.

substance.justification: No counterexample (e.g., topologist's sine curve is connected but not path-connected; rationals fail all three; etc.). Nothing mathematical at all.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF holds but the restriction would not have prevented gesturing at 'a shape made of two pieces with no path between' — the response simply doesn't try.

engagement.justification: Prompt is mereology (containment, exhaustion, non-overlap). Response is a Gettier-style argument about true belief vs. knowledge — adjacent philosophy but wrong topic; the three mereological conditions are not addressed.

coherence.justification: Internally coherent three-paragraph structure (first idea / what follows / evaluation) and each move follows from the prior; only the connection to the actual prompt fails.

substance.justification: Develops the JTB-vs-knowledge distinction with a small lucky-guess gesture, but this is irrelevant to wholes and parts; produces no counterexample for the actual definition.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads natively here ('right for the right reasons', 'lucky guess is not the same as a real way of seeing things'); the restriction doesn't hurt the (off-topic) argument.

engagement.justification: Prompt is a definition of trust requiring acquaintance, observed reliability, and reciprocity. Response is Kantian (ends-not-means) ethics — neighboring territory (interpersonal morality) but doesn't engage the three trust conditions.

coherence.justification: Three-step structure (first idea / second idea / evaluation) holds together; trails off mid-sentence at the end but the body coheres.

substance.justification: Develops the Kantian point reasonably (people have wants and a life), but this is the wrong argument; no trust counterexample (e.g., trusting a stranger on testimony violates (i); asymmetric trust violates (iii)).

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF carries the dignity-of-persons point well — 'a mind, a heart, and a need to be safe' is native-feeling restricted English.

engagement.justification: Prompt is set-cardinality via injective+surjective function. Response is about comparing two 'sets of words' on economic preferences and picking 'the best set' — no functions, no bijection, no cardinality.

coherence.justification: Sentences cycle: 'If one set is always better, that is the same way. If the first set is always better, then no set is always better. If both groups are the same, we do not need to pick one.' Self-contradictory and fragmentary.

substance.justification: Offers no counterexample (e.g., the empty function from {} to {a} is injective but not surjective and the sets differ in cardinality, illustrating necessity of (iii); or N vs. Z to challenge intuition). Pure non-engagement.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF surface holds but the restriction-plus-misreading produces a swirl of 'set/better/same' tokens with no mathematical content; argument breaks.
